text: Montaigne's essay 'On Experience' ends with a defense of the particular: each man's experience, if examined honestly, contains universal truth. This is why his solitude is never merely introspective navel-gazing. By attending scrupulously to his own digestion, moods, and reading habits, he was doing what he considered the hardest thing: observing without a framework borrowed from authority. The crowd supplies ready-made frameworks — religious, political, fashionable. Withdrawing from it was, in Montaigne's practice, the only way to encounter the texture of actual experience rather than the idea of experience one has been handed.
question: Why does Montaigne insist on observing one's own experience without borrowed frameworks?

options.text: To avoid the influence of corrupt religious institutions
Because universally valid truth can only be found in careful personal observation
To encounter actual experience rather than the idea of experience supplied by the crowd
Because personal experience is more reliable than classical philosophy
